danger_signals.meaning: active threat requiring player engagement or evasion
immediate danger to player avatar; damage imminent
player vulnerability increasing; failure state approaching

safety_signals.meaning: temporary respite; opportunity for repositioning or healing

safety_signals.visual_form: weapon pickups or ammo counter on HUD
safety_signals.meaning: capability to sustain offensive action; reduced vulnerability

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: player avatar/character sprites distinct from adversaries
uniformed or distinctly colored enemy sprites

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: arena populated with enemies; combat environment visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies actively attacking; player must engage or evade
brief pause before next enemy wave spawns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: HUD health bar near depletion; possible visual screen effects
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: increased enemy aggression or damage rates if alive

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: title screen or game over state displayed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: input disabled; cartridge anti-piracy check activated on title screen (SRAM mismatch detection)

environmental_storytelling.example: SNES port features multiple character options with different narrative context, differentiating from arcade original
Game logo and visual identity establish Operation Thunderbolt as tactical/military action scenario

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: projectile attacks toward player; movement patterns in arena
responsive to input; weapon discharge when triggered; movement across screen
continuous SRAM anti-piracy check on each frame; disables controller input if SRAM detected (piracy countermeasure)

notes: Operation Thunderbolt is a light-gun action game (Super Scope enhanced) with straightforward danger/safety communication through enemy presence and health state. The source material provided focuses on technical development artifacts and anti-piracy mechanisms rather than visual/behavioral manifestation systems. Visual communication patterns inferred from paradigm conventions of early 90s action games. The SNES port differs from arcade original in storyline and character selection, suggesting faction or narrative identity differentiation through playable character choice.
